entry.title: SAP Bussiness One FAT-Client UI Display Issues
entry.content:
Hello everyone,
SAP Support suggested I try posting this in the community, since others here might have encountered a similar issue and could possibly offer a solution.

The issue is that for some of our customers—only sporadically and not reproducibly—the main menu gets pushed together in a strange way.

We’ve seen this with several customers using different SAP 10 versions.

We’ve already tested a few things, such as:

- Set the scale to 100% (still the case)

- Set the high DPI setting to System in SAP. (As with Corsuit)

- Disabled Windows multitasking. (Because errors such as the entire bar turning black occurred)

- Set the refresh rate to 60 Hz

- Installed the latest drivers (Today, we uninstalled all drivers on a test computer and installed the drivers directly from Intel)

- As silly as it sounds, all the computers where this error occurred were connected via HDMI cable. We even changed that to DisplayPort.

- What we found is that computers with, for example, an Nvidia graphics card do not have this error. Only Intel processors with integrated graphics cards (different generations 12/13/14) have this error.

-This error also occurred on non-Lenovo devices such as a Surface during a presentation where it was connected to a projector. Reports were open there (ID 42/2419) customer order, which was opened from the shipping list. When the packing list was opened, the error occurred. (Unfortunately, when testing with the same items on the device, this could no longer be reproduced).

-This error also occurred on non-Lenovo devices such as a Surface during a presentation where it was connected to a projector. Reports were open there (ID 42/2419) customer order, which was opened from the shipping list. When the packing list was opened, the error occurred. (Unfortunately, when testing with the same items on the device, it could no longer be reproduced).

-We also set up other monitors with only 1080p resolution because we thought it might have something to do with the screen.

-We also disabled settings such as hardware GPU acceleration in Windows.

-I also checked the system utilization and the network card, which were always only around 40% utilization. The Surface was the only one where it was at 80% at the time.

-I also changed the energy-saving settings to high performance or best performance on the notebooks.

-I also checked the network settings. I disabled Wi-Fi so that they are only connected via LAN. I also set the card so that Windows cannot disable it for energy-saving options. I also adjusted the other energy-saving settings that are available there.

-I couldn't see anything unusual in the Windows event log during that time.

Almost all of our customers use the Coresuite Country Package. However, Coresystems was unable to reproduce the issue on their end.

Maybe someone else has an idea, or has encountered a problem like this before.

Thank you very much and best regards,
Marco

entry.title: The Role of Generative AI in SAP Testing: Opportunities and Limitations
entry.content:
Why SAP Testing Has Become More Complex

Modern SAP ecosystems comprise highly interlinked applications where workflows constantly interact with APIs, cloud computing solutions, databases, legacy systems, and other third-party software used in business processes. No longer can organizations limit their testing activities to testing modules or transaction screens. Instead, they need to assess workflow inter-dependencies, integration quality, process integrity, data integrity, and authorization schemes at once.

This complexity has significantly increased demand for enterprise-grade SAP QA testing services. It is necessary for businesses to test vital workflows, including Procure-to-Pay, Order-to-Cash, payroll, account reconciliation, and inventory management, without disturbing business operations. As SAP solutions tend to be updated on an ongoing basis, traditional testing approaches cannot cope with business requirements.

The importance of SAP functional testing has also increased considerably. Functional validation now requires broader coverage across customized workflows, regional business processes, and industry-specific operational requirements. In many enterprise environments, thousands of interconnected transactions and business rules must be validated before deployment.

Organizations also depend heavily on SAP regression testing services to confirm that updates, integrations, or configuration changes do not affect existing functionalities. As SAP ecosystems expand, regression cycles become larger, more repetitive, and increasingly difficult to manage manually. This is where generative AI is beginning to reshape enterprise quality engineering.

Opportunities Created by Generative AI in SAP Testing

Generative AI introduces intelligence into multiple stages of the SAP testing lifecycle by reducing repetitive activities and improving testing precision. Instead of depending entirely on manual scripting and validation, enterprises are using AI-driven models to improve testing scalability and execution efficiency.

According to industry discussions around generative AI adoption in SAP quality engineering, AI-powered systems can generate test cases, analyze business workflows, create synthetic test data, and identify high-risk process areas significantly faster than conventional testing methods. This allows organizations to execute broader validation cycles without proportionally increasing testing effort.

Key Contributions of Generative AI in SAP Testing

AreaTraditional SAP TestingSAP Testing Using Generative AI
Test Case CreationManual scripting and scenario preparation require substantial effort and time.AI generates test scenarios automatically based on workflows, business rules, and historical data.
Test Data ManagementTesting teams rely on static datasets that often require continuous maintenance.AI creates synthetic, production-like datasets for broader validation coverage.
Defect IdentificationDefect detection depends heavily on tester experience and limited test coverage.AI analyzes historical trends and identifies high-risk areas proactively.
Regression TestingLarge regression suites increase execution timelines and resource dependency.AI-driven prioritization improves efficiency across SAP regression testing services.
Automation MaintenanceFrequent SAP UI or workflow changes require script rewrites.AI-enabled self-healing mechanisms adjust scripts automatically.
Integration ValidationManual integration validation becomes difficult across hybrid environments.AI strengthens analysis across SAP integration testing services by identifying dependency risks earlier.
Execution SpeedTesting cycles are slower due to repetitive manual effort.AI accelerates validation cycles and supports continuous testing approaches.

Major Opportunities Enterprises Can Achieve with Generative AI

Reduced Testing Timelines: AI-driven automation significantly reduces the time required for test case generation, execution, and maintenance. This helps enterprises accelerate release cycles while maintaining validation accuracy.

Improved Testing Coverage: Generative AI can simulate multiple business conditions, workflows, and user interactions that are often missed during manual validation processes.

Higher Operational Efficiency: Organizations implementing SAP test automation services can reduce repetitive manual effort and improve productivity across large testing programs.

Better Release Stability: AI-powered analysis helps enterprises identify high-risk workflows earlier. This helps reduce post-deployment defects and operational disruptions.

Scalable Enterprise Validation: Businesses managing global SAP rollouts can execute large-scale testing programs more efficiently without proportionally increasing testing resources.

Limitations and Challenges of Generative AI in SAP Testing

Despite its advantages, generative AI cannot independently replace experienced SAP testing professionals. Enterprise SAP environments contain customized workflows, industry-specific configurations, compliance requirements, and operational dependencies that require a strong business understanding.

Major Limitations Enterprises Must Address

Dependency on Data Quality: AI systems depend heavily on historical datasets and workflow information. Incomplete or inconsistent data can generate inaccurate testing outputs and unreliable recommendations.

Limited Business Context Awareness: AI-generated test cases may overlook nuanced operational scenarios, conditional workflows, or regulatory exceptions. Human validation remains necessary for accurate business process assessment.

Governance and Compliance Risks: Enterprises operating in regulated industries require transparent and traceable testing processes. AI-generated outputs without governance controls may create audit and compliance concerns.

Integration Complexity: Many enterprises operate hybrid environments containing SAP applications, external platforms, APIs, and legacy systems. Integrating AI-driven testing capabilities into existing frameworks often requires infrastructure modernization and process redesign.

Human Oversight Remains Essential: AI can accelerate repetitive testing activities, but it cannot independently evaluate strategic business outcomes, operational continuity, or customer impact. Experienced testing teams remain critical for enterprise validation activities.

Final Say

Generation AI is revolutionizing enterprise quality engineering through better automation scalability, faster test creation, and effective defect analysis in complex SAP environments. With the continuous improvement of enterprise ERP infrastructure and the more widespread application of AI in enterprise functions, organizations are making increasing use of intelligent automation as part of their SAP testing services, which enables more efficient validation and prevents execution bottlenecks.

To fully capitalize on these advances, organizations must balance intelligent automation with robust business expertise and disciplined, governance-driven testing strategies. Success in SAP testing hinges on combining AI-driven speed and efficiency with experienced professionals who safeguard operational continuity, compliance, and business-critical processes. Enterprises should proactively integrate AI capabilities with structured quality engineering practices, ensuring their SAP environments remain resilient, trustworthy, and ready for future demands.

entry.title: Join the Espresso session on: Unlocking Energy Flexibility with SAP Distributed Energy Resources
entry.content:
Espresso Session | June 16, 2026 | 4:00 PM (CEST)

The energy landscape is undergoing a fundamental transformation. What was once a centralized system is rapidly evolving into a decentralized ecosystem driven by distributed energy resources (DER) and active prosumers.

In our upcoming Espresso Session on June 16 we will explore how utilities can successfully navigate this shift and unlock new business opportunities through SAP’s Distributed Energy Resources capabilities.

Why It Matters

DER—like rooftop solar, batteries, and EVs—are reshaping how energy is produced, consumed, and managed. While this introduces new challenges (data volume, grid stability, new market players), it also unlocks exciting possibilities:

  • Energy flexibility markets
  • New business models
  • Smarter, more resilient grids

How SAP Helps

SAP enables utilities to simplify complexity and stay ahead by:

  • Connecting data and processes end-to-end—from asset registration to billing
  • Enabling collaboration across the ecosystem (utilities, prosumers, operators)
  • Providing real-time insights for smarter decision-making

What You’ll Learn

In just 30 minutes, you’ll get:

  • SAP’s vision for DER and the decentralized energy future
  • A look at current solutions and the roadmap ahead
  • Real-world insights into managing prosumer and energy data
  • A preview of upcoming innovations

Who Should Join

This session is designed for professionals across the energy sector, including utilities, distribution system operators, and energy suppliers. It is particularly relevant for those working in areas such as asset registration, energy communities, behind-the-meter operations, congestion and capacity management, prosumer optimization, connectivity, data management, meter-to-cash, and flexibility services.

Anyone looking to better manage distributed energy resources and enable new energy business models will benefit from this session.

Ready to Get Started?

Distributed energy is transforming not just infrastructure, but value creation itself. Don’t miss the chance to stay ahead.

Join our session and explore what’s next for energy.
Our experts Mateu Munar and Stephane Dotto are excited to welcome you.

Register here >> Registration page!

Learn More

To explore the topic further, please refer to the following resources:

  • Read the SAP Community Blog: Driving the Future of Energy Flexibility with SAP Distributed Energy Resources
    SAP News Article: SAP Distributed Energy Resources Enables the Energy Flexibility Market

entry.title: New Course: Discovering the Autonomous Enterprise
entry.content:
🚀New Learning Opportunity: Discovering the Autonomous Enterprise

As organizations continue to navigate rapid digital transformation, the concept of the autonomous enterprise is quickly becoming a key driver of innovation, efficiency, and resilience. This course is designed to help you understand what that means and why it matters.

🌐What Is an Autonomous Enterprise?

An autonomous enterprise leverages advanced technologies such as AI, machine learning, and intelligent automation to run and optimize business processes with minimal human intervention. The goal? Faster decisions, improved efficiency, and the ability to continuously adapt in a changing world.

📚What You’ll Learn

In this course, you’ll gain:

  • A clear understanding of SAP's autonomous enterprise vision
  • Insights into how intelligent technologies transform business operations
  • Real-world examples of how automation and data-driven decision-making create value
  • An overview of the building blocks and key capabilities needed to get started

Whether you're new to the topic or looking to deepen your understanding, this course provides a solid foundation.

👥Who Should Take This Course?

This learning experience is designed for:

  • Business professionals exploring digital transformation
  • IT leaders and architects
  • SAP customers, partners, and consultants
  • Anyone curious about the future of intelligent enterprises

No advanced technical background is required, just curiosity and a willingness to learn.

💡Why It Matters

The shift toward autonomous enterprises isn’t a distant future, it’s happening now. Organizations that embrace intelligent automation are better equipped to:

  • Respond faster to market changes
  • Improve operational efficiency
  • Unlock new business opportunities

This course helps you understand how to be part of that transformation.

✅Get Started Today

Take the first step toward understanding the future of business innovation.

👉  Discovering the Autonomous Enterprise

entry.title: Re: Create Purchasing Contract with conditions via API Pu...
entry.content:
Hello @PatrickLin ,

Creating conditions from another system is possible. Here is how:

Let's say that you have a price of 13,37€, then (most likely) your default condition is PB00 (standard price) for SAP.

  • If you now want to add another 11% of discount to it, you can use RA00 (%-discount from net value) and pass the corresponding value

For the OData service, you would add both conditions to to_PurContrItemCndnValdty like so:

"to_PurContrItemCndnValdty": {
  "results": [
    {
      "ConditionType": "PB00",
      "to_PurContrItmCndnAmount": {
        "results": [
          {
            "ConditionType": "PB00",
            "ConditionCurrency": "EUR",
            "ConditionRateValue": "13.37"
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "ConditionType": "RA00",
      "to_PurContrItmCndnAmount": {
        "results": [
          {
            "ConditionType": "RA00",
            "ConditionCurrency": "%",
            "ConditionRateValue": "11"
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}

  Which results in the conditions being sent / added during creation to your SAP system:

However, item scales is not possible as @Lakshmipathi mentioned 😞 

  • I didn't debug, but I suppose it's a missing standard feature.

KR
Hermann

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Apps Deprecation and The Path Forward
entry.content:
Hi @MateusOliveiraInetum, sure, very sorry for missing it. Most likely you are referring to the homepage and further materials. It is still correct that we offer LCNC capabilities within SAP Build. Existing SAP Build Apps customers can use SAP Build Apps until their contract ends and we also ensure the maintenance, support, SLAs, etc.
What you still see on the homepage, etc. are first, the LCNC capabilities to create a process using SAP Build Process Automation. Second, there is a small part of SAP Build Apps included in the SAP Build offering. Please see here our product documentation.

I highly recommend watching the following to see the latest updates about our offering: 

  • Watch the replay of Michael Gall's Sapphire session The enterprise path to agentic AI
  • Read Michael Gall's blog post about Joule Studio: From Business Intent to Enterprise-Ready AI Agents

Best regards,
Patrick

entry.title: Getting Up to Speed with SAP Commerce Cloud Updates
entry.content:
Keeping your SAP Commerce Cloud environment current is a strategic decision, not only a maintenance routine. Many customers still find themselves uncertain about when to update, how the framework update affects their systems, or where to find the right release information at the right time. 

To address this, the Readiness@Scale team is happy to introduce a new e-learning guide: Getting Up to Speed with SAP Commerce Cloud Updates. This short, self-paced unit is available through SAP Learning. It is designed to take the guesswork out of staying current with SAP Commerce Cloud and help your organization understand the update process.  

Why This Guide Matters and Who It’s For 

This guide is intended for anyone involved in planning, managing, or advising on SAP Commerce Cloud environments, including: 

  • System administrators and operations teams - responsible for managing update cycles and release deployments. 
  • Solution architects and developers - preparing for the JDK 21 framework update and its technical dependencies. 
  • Business stakeholders and product owners - who need to understand the strategic value of staying current and want to actively contribute to shaping the product roadmap. 

To complete this guide, you do not need to have deep technical expertise. It is structured to be accessible to both technical and business-oriented roles within your organization.  With SAP Commerce Cloud 2211, SAP moved from infrequent, large-scale releases to a continuous innovation model with monthly releases. Customers are already familiar with receiving smaller, predictable updates each month, including security patches, bug fixes, and new features. The guide makes a compelling case for why this model benefits your business. 

What You Will Learn 

The learning asset is organized into four focused lessons that give you a complete picture of how to manage and benefit from SAP Commerce Cloud updates. 

  1. The update model and why it matters — Understand how the 2211 continuous innovation strategy works, including the monthly release cadence, Feature Rollout Phases, and why framework readiness is critical for security, compliance, and long-term support. 
  2. The framework update process — Walk through the structured migration plan from JDK 17 to JDK 21, with clear phases and deadlines running through August 2026. Learn what changes technically — Spring 6.x, OAuth, Drools 10 — and what that means for your environment. 
  3. Where to find release information — Discover the SAP Customer Experience Release Navigator as your central hub for release notes, roadmaps, and technical resources. Learn which communication channels to subscribe to, so you are never caught off guard by a change. 
  4. How to influence the product roadmap — Explore the SAP Continuous Influence Program and the practical ways your organization can submit ideas, vote on priorities, join beta testing, and engage directly with SAP and the broader community. 

Start Learning Today 

This guide is a practical, focused resource that equips both technical and business audiences with the knowledge to operate SAP Commerce Cloud. Whether you are preparing for the framework update, building an internal update strategy, or simply looking to understand the release model better, it is the right starting point.  Access this guide through SAP Learning  Getting Up to Speed with SAP Commerce Cloud Updates. A valid S-user ID and an active SAP Enterprise Support subscription are required to access the content. 

Bookmark the SAP Commerce Cloud release navigator page to stay informed about current changes. 

A Similar Guide for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud 

If your organization also works with SAP Sales Cloud or SAP Service Cloud, you can leverage the available e-learning guide Getting Up to Speed with the Releases of SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud. It follows the same approach and aims to help customers understand the release timeline and necessary activities during each stage from initial planning, validation in testing tenant to moment the customer system goes live with updates. Access the guide here: Getting Up to Speed with the Releases of SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud. A valid S-user ID and an active SAP Enterprise Support subscription are required to access the content.

Best Run,

Readiness@Scale, SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to completely delete a worker in SAP S...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

Have you already had a look at the following information?

Destroying Workforce Assignment Data

I also asked our internal chatbot for support. Here's the response; please check whether the information helps you solve the issue:

How to Delete a Worker in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Yes, it is possible to delete a worker in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but the process involves specific steps and configurations. Here is a step-by-step guide:

  1. Terminate Employment or Work Agreement:  You need to terminate the employment or work agreement in the source system where it was created. This can be done using the Manage Workforce app. See information in Human Resources - Data Management for HR Connectivity and Workforce Details.

  2. Schedule an SAP Information Lifecycle Management (ILM) Job: To delete employee data, you need to schedule an ILM job that periodically archives or deletes terminated employees. This process cannot be followed for a specific workforce person or a set of workforce persons. See information in 2922814 - How to Delete Employee Data in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

  3. Maintain ILM Policies and Rules: Set up the ILM policies and rules to define the retention period for employee data. Once the retention period is over, the ILM job will delete the data.

  4. Blocking and Deleting Business Users: Consider the consequences of deleting user master records, as it may affect references to that user master record.

  5. Archiving and Destroying Business Partner Data: Use the archiving object CA_BUPA to archive business partner data.

For more detailed information, you can refer to the SAP Help Portal documentation on ‘Data Management for HR Connectivity and Workforce Details’.

If the worker data was replicated from SAP SuccessFactors, you should manage deletions in SAP SuccessFactors and replicate the changes to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Note

  • The deletion process is primarily managed through SAP ILM, and it is not possible to delete a worker directly from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition without following these steps.
  • If you encounter inconsistencies, it is recommended to seek assistance from an SAP Product Support engineer.

#GenAI-Assisted Content

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: AI-Assisted Development in SAP Cloud Integration: Four Features You Should Know
entry.content:
SAP has been steadily building AI capabilities directly into SAP Integration Suite,         not as a standalone tool, but embedded into the workflows developers use every day. Whether you are creating a new integration flow, modeling individual steps, reviewing Groovy scripts, or connecting to AI services at runtime, there are now AI-powered features available at each of these stages. In this blog post I will walk through four of them that are generally available today in Cloud Integration, explain how each one works, and show you what to expect when you use them.

The features covered in this post:

  • Artifact Generation
  • Flow Design Proposal
  • Script Optimization
  • AI Adapter

 

Artifact Generation

Artifact Generation allows you to create a new Integration Flow by describing your integration scenario in natural language. The AI interprets your description, derives a suitable flow structure, and creates the artifact directly inside your Integration Package — giving you a properly structured starting point without having to build it step by step from an empty canvas.

To access the feature, open an Integration Package and add a new artifact. Instead of jumping straight into an empty editor, you are now presented with two options:

 

 

Select "Generate Integrations with assistance from AI" to open the input dialog. Describe your integration scenario in the text field — it accepts up to 1,000 characters. Descriptions must be written in English; other languages are not supported. The more precise your description is, specifying source system, target system, data format, and the key process steps — the better the result will be.

The AI validates your input immediately. If the description is too vague or does not contain enough information to derive a meaningful flow, it returns a message asking you to refine it. Once the description is sufficient, the AI confirms this, suggests an artifact name, and lets you select the sender and receiver system before generating:

 

 

 

 

 

After clicking Generate, the iFlow is created in the package and opens directly in the Integration Flow Designer. The result is a complete flow structure with all relevant process steps already in place:

 

 

It is important to understand what the AI actually delivers here. The generated iFlow provides the full structural skeleton: correct step sequence, meaningful component names, sender and receiver channels, required converters and loggers, and an Exception Subprocess. What it does not provide is the implementation of any individual step. Adapter configurations, message mappings, Groovy scripts, and error handling logic all still need to be configured manually.

The value of the feature lies in eliminating the setup work at the start of a new integration project: deciding which steps are needed, getting them in the right order, and establishing a consistent naming structure. That groundwork is now done in seconds.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Premium Edition, not in Standard Edition.

 

Flow Design Proposal

The Flow Design Proposal feature, also referred to as Recommended Steps, provides AI-guided suggestions while you are actively modeling an iFlow. Rather than browsing the full step catalog every time you want to add a component, the AI analyzes the current state of your flow and recommends the most suitable next steps directly in the Add Flow Step dialog:

 

The recommendations appear automatically at the top of the dialog whenever you add a new step. The AI takes into account the adapter types already configured, the message format expected at the current position in the flow, and common design patterns for the type of integration being modeled. Each suggestion includes the component type and a short explanation of why it fits at this point.

Clicking a recommendation inserts the component directly at the correct position in the flow. The suggestions update with every step you add, adapting to the evolving state of the flow.

The full step catalog remains available below the Recommended Steps section at all times the feature is advisory, not restrictive. For experienced developers it speeds up the modeling process; for developers newer to Cloud Integration it provides meaningful guidance on which steps belong in which context.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Enhanced Edition and on Standard Edition.

 

Script Optimization

The Script Optimizer is an AI-powered code review feature built directly into the Groovy script editor in Cloud Integration. It analyzes an existing script against SAP Cloud Integration best practices, identifies quality issues and upgrade readiness problems, and generates both a detailed review report and a ready-to-use improved version of the script. The feature is accessible via the Optimize button in the top-right corner of the script editor.

Clicking Optimize triggers the analysis. The editor switches to a split view — the original script remains on the left, while the AI-Generated Review Report appears on the right:

 

 

The report gives a structured breakdown of all identified issues. Each finding is numbered, named, and explained in detail — what the problem is, why it matters in the context of SAP Cloud Integration, and what needs to change. Typical findings include KBA violations such as loading the full message payload into memory as a String instead of using a streaming interface, missing error handling for JSON parsing, inefficient null checks, and patterns that are not compatible with current Groovy versions.

 

 

At the end of the report, the AI provides a fully rewritten version of the script that addresses all identified issues:

The improved script can be copied and applied directly in the editor. The report itself can also be downloaded, which is useful for documentation purposes or when reviewing changes with a team.

 

 

The Problems panel below the editor provides two tabs — Upgrade Readiness Checks and General Script Checks — giving a structured overview of all findings before stepping through the full report.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Enhanced Edition, not on Standard Edition.

 

 

AI Adapter

The AI Adapter is a dedicated, native adapter type in SAP Integration Suite that enables an Integration Flow to call an AI service as a standard step within the integration process. Rather than building custom HTTP calls to AI backends, the adapter provides a structured, purpose-built configuration for AI service communication — making capabilities such as text generation, content analysis, and classification a native part of your integration flows.

The adapter is available in the standard adapter type list when adding a receiver channel in the Integration Flow Designer:

 

 

 

After selecting AI as the adapter type, you choose the message protocol that matches your target AI service:

 

 

  • Two variants are available. AICore-GenAIHub connects to SAP AI Core via the Generative AI Hub — the right choice when working within the SAP BTP ecosystem. Custom-OpenAI-REST connects to any OpenAI-compatible REST endpoint, covering custom model deployments or third-party providers that follow the OpenAI API specification.
  • Connection Tab

    The Connection tab defines how the adapter authenticates against and reaches the AI service.

    Address — the API endpoint URL of the AI service. For SAP AI Core, this is the API URL from your AI Core service key. For a custom deployment, it is the base URL of your OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

    OAuth2 Client Credentials — references a credential entry stored in the Integration Suite security material (Monitor → Security Material). You create this entry beforehand using the client ID and client secret from your AI Core service key, or the API key for your custom endpoint. The adapter uses this to authenticate each request.

    Connection Timeout / Response Timeout — both default to 60,000 ms. Depending on prompt complexity and model response time, it may be necessary to increase these values.

 

 

 


The Processing tab defines what is sent to the AI service and how the call is structured.

Operation: set to Orchestration - Completion, the standard mode for sending a prompt and receiving a generated text response.

Orchestration Deployment ID: the deployment ID of your AI scenario in SAP AI Core, available in the AI Core cockpit once a deployment has been created and started. It identifies which model configuration handles the request.

AI Resource Group:  the resource group within SAP AI Core, typically default unless dedicated groups have been configured for different use cases or teams.

Request Payload Source: UI Configurable means the prompt is defined directly within the adapter. The alternative passes the prompt dynamically via the incoming message, which is useful when the prompt content changes at runtime.

Model: the language model to use, for example gpt-4o-mini. Available models depend on what has been deployed in your AI Core instance or what your custom endpoint supports.

Role: the role context for the prompt. user represents the party sending the request, assistant the AI persona. For most integration scenarios, user is the appropriate choice.

Input: the prompt text sent to the AI model. The placeholder ${body} injects the current message payload dynamically into the prompt at runtime. This allows integration data — an error message, a purchase order, a customer record — to be passed directly to the AI without any additional scripting.

Availability: SAP Integration Suite Enhanced Edition and on Standard Edition.

 

Summary

The four features described in this post address AI support across the full iFlow development lifecycle. Artifact Generation provides a structured starting point for new integrations. Flow Design Proposal guides step selection during active modeling. Script Optimization delivers automated code review and improvement for existing Groovy scripts. The AI Adapter enables AI service calls as a native runtime step within any integration flow.

All four features are part of the Premium Edition of SAP Integration Suite and are generally available. Further AI capabilities — including the AI Message Monitoring Agent, Agent Orchestration, and the Error Analyzer — will be covered in a follow-up post.

entry.title: Re: Substitution Journal Entries - requirement for WBS
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

I asked our internal chatbot for support and here's the response. Please check whether the information helps you solve the issue:

Workaround for Substitution Issue in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, substituting postings to specific cost centers when the original posting is to a WBS element can be challenging due to the limitations in the substitution functionality. Here are some potential workarounds:

  1. Use Additional Attributes in Preconditions:

    • Enhance your substitution logic by using more attributes in the preconditions to ensure that the substitution only occurs under specific conditions. This can help avoid undesired statistical postings to both the cost center and WBS. Please see 3049565 - Work Item field populated for supplier invoice documents when the account assignment is cost center.

  2. Custom Business Objects:

    • Consider defining a custom table using the app Custom Business Objects. This table can map WBS elements to cost centers, and you can use table lookup functionality in your substitution rules to derive the correct cost center based on the WBS element. Please see Profit Center Derivation in Sales Documents - SAP Community,

  3. Manual Adjustments:

    • If automation is not feasible, manual adjustments might be necessary. After the initial posting, review and adjust the entries to ensure they meet the desired accounting structure.

  4. Consult SAP Support:

    • Since this functionality is not currently available, continue to engage with SAP Support and monitor any updates or feature requests that might address this limitation in future releases.

Unfortunately, the ideal solution of clearing the WBS value and posting directly to the substituted cost center is not available in the current design of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

#GenAI-Assisted Content

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Batch invoicing job for delivery plus sales order wit...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question and sorry for the delay.

I asked our internal chatbot for support and here's the response. Please check whether the information helps you solve the issue:

Automated Invoicing Routine in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

To implement an automated invoicing routine in SAP S/4HANA Cloud that creates billing documents based on Sales Orders and Outbound Deliveries, you can follow these steps:

Steps to Implement

  1. Use the Schedule Billing Document Request Creation App: 

    • This app allows you to create billing document requests (BDRs) through scheduled jobs. However, it seems that the parameters in this app do not fully meet your requirements. For more information, see Parallel Processing of Scheduling BDR Creation - SAP Community.

  2. Custom Logic with BAdIs:

    • You can use Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) to introduce custom logic  for billing processes. Specifically, the BAdI SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER can be used to control how data is copied from reference documents to billing documents. This can help ensure that each Sales Order and Outbound Delivery pair results in a separate billing document. For more information, see Sales - Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) for Sales Billing,

  3. Batch Invoicing Job:

    • Implement a batch job that processes deliveries and sales orders with the same WBS element or linked sales order for the sales organization. This can be done by customizing the logic in the BAdI to ensure that invoices are created per delivery.

  4. Output Type Configuration:

    • After creating the billing document, update the output type to EDI = Immediately. This can be configured in the Output Parameter Determination app.

  5. Avoiding Customer PO Reference:

    • Ensure that the routine does not use the customer PO as a reference to avoid combining multiple outbounds into a single invoice.

Considerations

  • Custom Fields and Logic: You may need to create custom fields to store specific identifiers like WBS elements and use them in your custom logic.
  • Testing: Thoroughly test the routine to ensure it meets the business requirements and does not cause issues with customer acceptance.

Image

#GenAI-Assisted Content

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: F2173 “Add Time Confirmation” requires Personnel Number while IW41 does not – Standard behavior?
entry.content:
Hello, 

I am currently testing the fiori App F2173 – Maintenance Orders and Operations on an SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 on-premise system. I found a behavior difference between the Fiori app and the classic GUI transactions regarding time confirmations. When I create a time confirmation from GUI (IW41), works correctly even if no personnel number is specified. From Fiori App F2173, using "Time Confirmations" --> "Add Time Confirmation", the system behaves differently:

  • If I specify a Personnel Number → confirmation is posted successfully
  • If I leave Personnel Number empty → error IW803 is raised: 

Observations:

- The maintenance order does NOT use the phase model

My question is this: 

 

  • expected standard behavior of F2173?
  • a known restriction/design difference versus IW41?
  • or potentially a bug/configuration issue?

Any clarification, SAP Notes, or design documentation references would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I
entry.content:
"One TCP Event Smart Adapter for multiple receiver types, security requirements and native Avro conversion to XML or JSON based on the Schema Registry"

Part I — The Journey & Architecture Overview

Bookmark this page to follow the entire journey.

A note on how this was built: This adapter was developed with the assistance of AI (Claude, Gemini, and ChatGPT).

However, the architecture decisions, component design, integration patterns, and all production code were validated, tested, and signed off by me personally.

The adapter is already running in a real SAP Integration Suite tenant with live Kafka traffic. AI accelerated the development velocity — the engineering judgment and validation are entirely human.

Built with: SAP Adapter Development Kit (ADK) 2.25.0 · Apache Camel 3.14.7 · Java 8 (compiled with JDK 17, --release 8)

· IntelliJ IDEA · Apache Kafka Client 3.x.

Introduction

In the world of Event-Driven Architecture (EDA), real-time, high-volume data streams between SAP landscapes and Apache Kafka clusters have become a core business requirement. While the standard SAP Integration Suite connectivity offers great foundations, complex enterprise scenarios often demand advanced capabilities regarding data governance, custom resilience strategies, and dynamic security handshakes.

To support the community and bridge these gaps, I developed a fully custom, open-source enterprise-grade Kafka Adapter using the SAP Adapter Development Kit (ADK). The adapter is already fully operational for core processing requirements, basic validation loops, and dynamic Apache Avro conversions. However, because corporate infrastructure is deeply varied, it is currently undergoing an extensive evaluation matrix across multi-tenant security landscapes.

This article marks the beginning of a 7-part deep-dive series where we will explore every architectural decision, threading model, and implementation detail of this component.

Why Build a Custom Adapter ?

My journey toward this adapter did not start from zero. It began with two published architectural patterns for Kafka integration within SAP BTP Integration Suite — patterns that the SAP community embraced and that I am proud to have contributed:

 

  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Event Routing per Domain with Metadata-Driven Consumer Orchestration
  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI

While designing and validating those patterns in real enterprise environments, I began hitting the boundaries of what the standard SAP Kafka adapter was designed to do. These were not edge cases — they were recurring constraints that architects and developers face every day when trying to build serious event-driven integrations on SAP BTP.

The most frustrating part was not the limitations themselves. It was watching integration teams work around them by proliferating custom Groovy scripts and external libraries directly inside their iFlows just to deserialize an Avro payload, resolve a schema, or handle a partition assignment. That approach works — until it doesn't. Maintenance becomes a nightmare, governance disappears, and the iFlow starts carrying responsibilities it was never designed to carry.

That is when I made the decision: move the complexity where it belongs — into the adapter layer — and give the SAP global community a proper open-source solution.

What the standard adapter typically lacks, and what this custom adapter addresses directly:

  • Granular offset management — NACK-equivalent behavior via seek(), ensuring no message is silently lost on processing failure regardless of where in the batch the error occurred
  • Structured error visibility — purpose-built visual error blocks in the CPI monitoring layer, not raw unreadable stack traces that leave operators guessing at 2am
  • A multi-profile security engine — UI that adapts dynamically based on the chosen authentication strategy: SASL Basic, Certificate-only mTLS, or OAuth 2.0 Advanced — all consolidated in a single Connection tab with zero ambiguity
  • Native Avro conversion with Schema Registry governance — runtime deserialization of binary Avro payloads directly in the adapter, eliminating the need for Groovy deserializers inside iFlows. And yes, it has real-world limits: payload conversion supports up to 2 MB per record and Schema Registry wire headers up to 20 KB. Anything beyond those boundaries triggers a clear exception with an actionable error message — no silent failures, no data corruption
  • Configurable resilience policies — Skip, Stop, Retry with exponential backoff, and Skip Oversized, each implementing the correct Kafka offset semantics so your consumer group never gets stuck on a poison pill
  • On-premise connectivity — native Cloud Connector support via SAP SCC Location ID, covering enterprise landscapes where Kafka clusters sit behind corporate network boundaries
  • Explicit partition selection — direct partition assignment for use cases requiring deterministic consumption without relying on consumer group rebalancing

This is not a replacement for the SAP standard adapter for simple scenarios. It is an extension of it — built by someone who has spent real time in the trenches of SAP Kafka integration, for the engineers and architects who face the same walls I did.

Figure 1 — SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture): In this pattern, the Event Smart Kafka Adapter serves as the single inbound channel from a central Apache Kafka bus — which itself may receive events from TCP Kafka producers, AMQP bridges (RabbitMQ / ActiveMQ), MQTT brokers, or HTTPS webhooks upstream. The adapter handles topic subscription, protocol normalization, message validation, metadata extraction, and error handling before delivering clean events into SAP CPI. Inside the Integration Suite, the orchestration layer takes over: content enrichment, dynamic routing, message transformation, and domain-aware dispatch to SAP S/4HANA, SAP ECC, Web Services, Cloud Applications, Databases, and Object Storage — governed by ODCP/EDCP contracts and centralized security. License: Apache 2.0 | MIT — SDIA Framework: CC BY 4.0.

What to Expect: The 7-Part Deep-Dive Journey

To fully unpack the engineering behind an enterprise-grade SAP adapter, this initiative is structured into seven comprehensive technical chapters:

Part

Title

Focus

I ★

The Journey & Architecture Overview

Project vision, metadata configuration, visual logging frameworks, and open-source delivery roadmap. (This article)

II

Core Consumer Lifecycles & Polling Loops

Java runtime deep dive: how the Apache Camel polling consumer lifecycle maps to Kafka cluster subscription models.

III

The Multi-Profile Security Engine

Technical breakdown of SASL, mTLS, and OAuth 2.0 dynamic token integration using the SAP Secure Store.

IV

Multi-Threaded Parallel Consumers & Scale

Engineering for high-throughput. Parallel threading pools inside Cloud Integration worker nodes without JVM memory leaks.

V

Advanced Payload Conversion & Schema Registry Governance

Avro-to-JSON/XML serialization layer, caching strategies, and memory protection mechanisms.

VI

Designing the Producer (Receiver) Integration Role

Outbound flow: partition keys, acknowledgment levels (acks), and asynchronous message dispatching.

VII

Visual Operations, Error Interception & Production Sign-Off

Custom error UI rendering, transaction boundary management, and production go-live best practices.

Note: Source code repository, GitHub links, and downloadable .esa package will be published progressively alongside each milestone. Part I focuses on architecture and design decisions — code drops begin with Part II.

A First Look: The Adapter Running Live in SAP Integration Suite

Before diving into the configuration details, here is a quick spoiler — the adapter is not a concept or a prototype. It is already deployed and running in a real SAP BTP Integration Suite tenant, consuming live Kafka events.

Event Smart Kafka Adapter deployed as a custom adapter in SAP Integration Suite — iFlow started, Sender channel active, consuming live events from a Confluent Cloud Kafka topic.

No more Groovy deserializers inside your iFlow. The adapter resolves the Avro schema from an external Schema Registry at runtime and delivers clean JSON directly to the pipeline — conversion happens at the channel layer, before your integration logic even starts.

SAP Cloud Integration Operations Monitor showing live message processing — green checkmarks confirming successful Avro-to-JSON conversion and pipeline delivery.

The Dynamic Configuration UI

One of the most important aspects of an enterprise-grade adapter is its configuration experience. The adapter ships with a fully declarative metadata-driven UI built on the SAP ADK Component Metadata specification. Every tab, every field, and every visibility condition is defined in XML — no UI code required.

Connection Tab — Authentication Profiles

The Connection tab dynamically adapts based on the selected Authentication Method. Selecting one of three profiles completely changes the visible fields, ensuring operators are never confused by irrelevant configuration options.

Dynamic UI showing Kafka Cluster Bootstrap Servers table and SASL Basic configuration: TLS toggle, SASL Mechanism (PLAIN / SCRAM-SHA-256 / SCRAM-SHA-512), and Credential Name from SAP Secure Store.

 Automated UI adaptation showing mutual TLS configuration: Certificate Alias from SAP Keystore and optional mTLS Client Key Alias for bidirectional certificate authentication.

 Advanced token-based authentication layout: Token Endpoint URL, Client ID, Client Secret Alias (resolved from SAP Secure Store), and optional OAuth Scope — compatible with Confluent Cloud OAuth and AWS MSK IAM.

 

Connection Tab — Proxy Type selector drives the network routing strategy. Internet mode connects directly to cloud-hosted Kafka brokers. On-Premise mode routes traffic through SAP Cloud Connector (SCC) via Location ID, enabling hybrid enterprise topologies where Kafka clusters live behind corporate firewalls — no VPN, no firewall exceptions, no public broker exposure required.

 Processing Tab — Stream Tuning & Resilience

The Processing tab exposes fine-grained Kafka consumer controls alongside a powerful Error Handling policy engine. The adapter supports four distinct resilience policies, each implementing the correct Kafka offset semantics:

  • Skip Failed Message — commit offset, continue processing. Record is intentionally discarded and the consumer advances to the next message without interruption.
  • Stop on Error — do NOT commit offset (NACK equivalent via seek()), consumer halts immediately. The failed record is re-delivered after iFlow fix and redeploy.
  • Retry Failed Message — exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s... capped at 30s), 5–10 configurable attempts. On exhaustion: NACK + consumer halt. No poison-pill infinite loops.
  • Skip Oversized — when payload exceeds the configured size limit, bypass conversion and pass raw bytes with warning headers downstream instead of blocking the entire consumer.
  • Partition Selection — explicit partition assignment for deterministic consumption. When configured, the consumer uses assign() instead of subscribe(), bypassing consumer group rebalancing entirely. Valid only for single-topic configurations.
  • Group ID — custom consumer group identifier for coordinated consumption across iFlow instances. When left empty, the adapter derives a deterministic group ID from the broker host and topic combination — ensuring stable group membership across redeployments without manual configuration.
  • Auto Offset Reset — controls consumer behavior when no committed offset exists for the group:
    • earliest — start from the very first available message in the topic, consuming the full history
    • latest — start from the next new message only, ignoring historical records
    • none — throw an exception if no committed offset is found, forcing explicit offset management

 Conversion & Conversion Advanced Tabs

The Conversion tab controls how Kafka record payloads are transformed before delivery to the iFlow pipeline. The adapter supports five inbound payload modes:

  • None (raw bytes) — payload delivered exactly as-is directly to the iFlow pipeline. Works transparently with any topic format: JSON, XML, plain text, or binary. No Schema Registry required, no conversion overhead. The simplest and most compatible mode.
  • Avro → JSON — binary Avro payload deserialized at the adapter layer using an external Schema Registry. The adapter resolves the schema, decodes the binary record, and delivers clean structured JSON to the iFlow. No Groovy, no custom libraries, no deserialization logic inside your integration flow.
  • Avro → XML — same runtime deserialization pipeline as Avro → JSON, but the output is delivered as XML — ready for SAP-native processing, IDoc mapping, or SOAP transformations without additional conversion steps.
  • JSON Schema (pass-through) — payload is already valid JSON. The adapter sets the correct Content-Type: application/json header and schema mode metadata, ensuring downstream mapping steps receive properly typed content without touching the payload body.
  • Protobuf → JSON (experimental) — planned for a future release. Currently passes raw bytes with a warning header. Full Protobuf deserialization support is on the roadmap.

 Schema ID Resolution — how the adapter finds the right Avro schema:

When Avro conversion is enabled, the adapter must resolve the correct schema from the Schema Registry before decoding the payload. Two resolution strategies are supported:

  • Confluent Magic Byte — the schema ID is embedded directly in the Kafka record payload following the Confluent wire format: 1 magic byte (0x00) + 4-byte big-endian schema ID + Avro binary data. The adapter reads the first 5 bytes, extracts the schema ID automatically, and fetches the schema from the registry at runtime. This is the standard behavior when producers use the Confluent Avro Serializer.
  • Fixed Schema ID — the schema ID is configured statically in the adapter channel. Requires three fields: Registry Host (the Schema Registry URL), Credential Name (alias from SAP Secure Store for registry authentication), and Schema ID (the integer identifier of the registered schema). Use this mode when payloads do not carry the Confluent wire format header, or when consuming from producers that do not use the standard Confluent serializer.

Conversion Tab with Avro → JSON selected, Schema ID Source set to Fixed Schema ID — Registry Host, Credential Name from SAP Secure Store, and static Schema ID configured. The dropdown also exposes the Confluent Magic Byte option for wire-format-aware producers.

Visual Operations Monitoring & Custom Error Handling

Enterprise adapters must not fail silently, nor should they spit unreadable, raw stack traces into the Cloud Integration web console. This implementation introduces a structured, high-visibility error block designed to pinpoint exactly where a stream disrupted — separating downstream processing issues from fatal schema serialization failures or Schema Registry connection timeouts — and explicitly lists actionable steps for operational teams.

Every error condition in the adapter is classified, enriched, and rendered as a structured visual block directly in the SAP Cloud Integration Operations Monitor. The block always contains:

  • The error category and root cause (Schema Registry 404, auth failure, payload too large, network timeout...)
  • The exact Kafka topic, partition, and offset where the failure occurred
  • The Schema ID that was being resolved at the time of failure
  • The raw HTTP response body from the Schema Registry when applicable
  • A step-by-step correction guide for the operations team

When something goes wrong, the adapter speaks clearly. Structured visual error block rendered directly in the CPI monitoring layer — root cause, exact Kafka offset, Schema Registry HTTP response, and step-by-step correction guide. No guesswork.

Every screenshot above was captured from a live tenant. The adapter handles real Kafka traffic, real Avro payloads, and real Schema Registry interactions. What you see in this series is not a lab exercise — it is production-grade engineering built for the SAP global community.

 Open Source Delivery Strategy & Ecosystem Roadmap

This project is built for the community. The distribution strategy aims to balance ease-of-use with open-source compliance:

Complete Source Code Repository

The upcoming public repository will contain the complete Java source code, fully stripped of any proprietary SAP libraries or confidential JAR dependencies. Anyone who wants to clone, modify, or compile the adapter from scratch will simply need to add their licensed SAP ADK dependencies into the pom.xml.

Ready-to-Deploy Package (.ESA)

For integration developers who want a plug-and-play experience without compiling code, a fully assembled .esa (Enterprise Software Archive) package will be provided. Download it and deploy directly into your SAP Cloud Integration tenant in seconds — no build tools required.

Milestone

Target Deliverable

Focus Content & Release Scope

Late June - 2026

Core Architecture & Code Drops

Publication of Parts I, II, and III. Release of the baseline Consumer source code and the plug-and-play .esa package for public testing.

July  - 2026

Enterprise Scaling & Security Modules

Publication of Parts IV, V, VI, and VII. Release of the advanced Producer code, OAuth 2.0 / mTLS validation modules, and the final visual operations integration framework.

The journey has just begun.

Community & Collaboration

Building this was a long journey, but testing it against the vast diversity of enterprise Kafka configurations is a collective task. I am releasing this as a living project. If you have unique requirements—be it specific Avro compatibility versions, niche security protocols, or complex multi-tenant partitioning needs—please reach out. Let’s make this the standard, robust, community-owned Kafka solution for SAP Integration Suite. Follow this series to save the project updates and join the community as a contributor.

Stay tuned for Part II, where we will dive straight into the code to examine the Consumer Lifecycles, Kafka Polling Loops, and Apache Camel Threading Models that make this adapter production-ready for enterprise SAP landscapes.

"This was built entirely outside of any corporate engagement — no vendor sponsorship, no SAP endorsement. Just an engineer who saw a gap and decided to fill it."

Ricardo Viana
Independent Solo Researcher

Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP

ORCID: 0009-0009-9549-5862

#SAPIntegrationSuite  #Kafka  #EventDrivenArchitecture  #OpenSource  #SAPCommunity  #SAPADK  #CloudIntegration  #Avro  #SchemaRegistry

entry.title: Re: find sql anywhere development files

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 Beta Program – Program finished (update from 28th of November 2025)
entry.content:
Hello @JarKey1,
if the external browser window is opened from ABAP using CL_GUI_HTML_VIEWER=>DETACH_URL_IN_BROWSER, then in the future you can decide to use the default browser (whatever that is). See SAP Note 2913405 - "SAP GUI for Windows: Dependencies to browsers / browser controls" to understand which mechanisms exist to open browser windows.
What everybody of course needs to keep in mind is that if the default browser is not supported by the applications you want to run (for example because the UI framework does not support the browser), then using the default browser would be a bad choice.
Best regards,
Frank

entry.title: Re: Why SAP S/4HANA Transformation Programs Fail Operationally Even When the Technology Works
entry.content:
Great write-up. The biggest takeaway for me is that S/4HANA programs don’t usually fail because of the software itself—they fail when operational readiness, change management, and governance lag behind the technical go-live.

What stood out most was the gap between a successful implementation and a successful day-2 operation: support teams were underprepared, users kept following old processes, and escalation paths were unclear. That’s a useful reminder that transformation has to be owned end-to-end, not treated as an IT-only project.

entry.title: Re: Need to turn off specific Ariba Supplier Notification...
entry.content:
I'm assuming you are referring to notifications generated from SAP Business Network to suppliers when your ERP sends a PO to the suppliers SBN account. You, can a buyer, cannot change the notification or delivery preferences for a supplier account (as they own that account). If your suppliers are getting confused by these notifications, your options are unfortunately limited to:

a) Instruct your suppliers to disable notifications from their Test accounts when you onboard them in Production.

b) Change your PO output condition records in your ECC test environment(s) to prevent sending POs to SBN Test accounts once you onboard them in Production. If there's targeted testing requiring supplier activity in the Test account, you could temporarily and selectively reenable the output conditions to send POs to them via SBN.

entry.title: ‌🇫🇷‌ Webinar: USF French SAP User Group on HCM
entry.content:
The French SAP User Group invites its members to a remote session dedicated to recent and upcoming developments in Human Capital Management for France.

The meeting will provide a structured update on the product roadmap, regulatory topics, and operational feedback, with a particular focus on payroll and DSN-related changes. It is intended for SAP Local Product Managers involved in HCM France who seek to stay informed about current institutional decisions, ongoing transitions, and practical insights from recent experiences.

Agenda

  1. “Fait générateur” (use contribution rates and thresholds of the for-period for active employees) : News / Transition / Developments
  2. Legal Changes including Minimum wage, Additional birth leave
  3. Other HCM France Update including : RPURATEATF0, New configuration option for contributions in DSN for Blocks 78, 79 and 82
  4. Reminders and Best Practices
  5. DSN 2027
  6. Q/A 

The event will be conducted in French.

REGISTRATION LINK

For expert insights into HCM topics in France, consider joining the France – Human Capital Management Localization Work Zone group.

entry.title: Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Require a Different Mindset Than Traditional ERP Implementations
entry.content:
Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Require a Different Mindset Than Traditional ERP Implementations

Introduction

One thing I’ve consistently observed across SAP S/4HANA transformation programs is that organizations often underestimate how different these programs are from traditional ERP implementations.

Many teams initially approach S/4HANA projects using the same delivery mindset they used for earlier ERP upgrades:

  • migrate the data,

  • validate integrations,

  • complete testing,

  • and go live.

Technically, those activities still matter. But S/4HANA transformations usually impact much more than technology alone.

In large enterprise programs, S/4HANA changes:

  • operational processes,

  • reporting structures,

  • governance models,

  • data ownership,

  • cross-functional workflows,

  • and decision-making visibility across the organization.

That’s why I’ve found that successful S/4HANA programs require a much broader transformation mindset instead of a traditional “system replacement” approach.

SAP ECC Upgrade Mindset vs SAP S/4HANA Transformation Mindset

Illustrative comparison showing how SAP S/4HANA transformations extend beyond traditional ERP upgrades by emphasizing business transformation, process redesign, data governance, and long-term value realization.

S/4HANA Programs Are Business Transformations, Not Just Technical Projects

One of the biggest differences with S/4HANA is the level of operational impact across the enterprise.

In traditional ERP upgrades, organizations often tried to preserve existing processes as much as possible. With S/4HANA, businesses usually revisit:

  • process standardization,

  • operational efficiency,

  • reporting simplification,

  • automation opportunities,

  • and governance alignment.

This creates both opportunity and complexity.

In one transformation program I supported, the technical migration itself progressed relatively smoothly. But business teams struggled because operational workflows changed more significantly than expected. Several regional teams continued following legacy approval paths that no longer aligned with the redesigned S/4HANA processes.

The challenge wasn’t system instability.
It was organizational adaptation.

Why Data Becomes More Important in S/4HANA

Another major shift in S/4HANA programs is the importance of data governance.

In many legacy ERP environments, organizations operate with:

  • duplicate master data,

  • inconsistent naming standards,

  • fragmented ownership,

  • and regional process variations.

These issues often remain manageable until transformation programs begin consolidating processes across business units.

I’ve seen situations where migration teams initially focused only on technical extraction and loading activities, while deeper master data inconsistencies surfaced much later during reconciliation and reporting validation.

S/4HANA transformations expose operational data problems very quickly because the platform increases visibility and process integration across the enterprise.

That’s why early profiling, validation, and governance alignment become extremely important.

Illustrative Data Quality Findings During SAP S/4HANA Migration

Illustrative example showing common data quality challenges identified during SAP S/4HANA migration programs, including duplicate master data, missing attributes, inconsistent classifications, and mapping variations. Sample data shown for educational purposes only.

The Importance of Cross-Functional Alignment

One lesson I’ve repeatedly learned is that technical success alone does not guarantee transformation success.

The most stable S/4HANA programs usually involve strong coordination between:

  • business teams,

  • migration teams,

  • infrastructure groups,

  • testing leads,

  • security teams,

  • and operational stakeholders.

When workstreams operate independently, even small communication gaps can create downstream issues during testing and cutover preparation.

In one program, reporting teams and migration teams used different reconciliation assumptions during mock cycles. Individually, both teams believed their results were correct. But because the reconciliation logic was not fully aligned, leadership received conflicting readiness updates.

That issue delayed decision-making until the governance team standardized the reporting methodology.

Experiences like that reinforced how important centralized coordination becomes in large SAP programs.

Why Mock Cycles Matter More Than Most Organizations Expect

Many organizations underestimate the value of repeated mock migration cycles.

From my experience:

  • early cycles expose structural issues,

  • middle cycles validate remediation,

  • later cycles improve operational confidence and predictability.

The smoothest cutovers I’ve participated in were usually supported by multiple iterative validation cycles instead of relying on a single “final test.”

Repeated cycles help organizations:

  • stabilize reconciliation processes,

  • identify recurring defects,

  • improve cutover timing,

  • validate operational readiness,

  • and reduce uncertainty before go-live.

Illustrative example showing how repeated mock migration cycles improve reconciliation accuracy, data quality, and operational readiness throughout an SAP S/4HANA transformation program. Sample metrics are for educational purposes only.

This becomes especially important in multi-region S/4HANA deployments where process dependencies are more complex.

S/4HANA Increases Visibility Across the Enterprise

One positive change I’ve seen with S/4HANA is improved operational visibility.

Organizations gain stronger insight into:

  • transactional consistency,

  • reporting accuracy,

  • operational bottlenecks,

  • integration dependencies,

  • and process standardization opportunities.

Illustrative SAP S/4HANA Operational Visibility Dashboard

Illustrative example showing how SAP S/4HANA enables centralized operational visibility across finance, supply chain, sales, inventory, and enterprise reporting functions. Sample metrics are for demonstration purposes only and do not represent customer data.

But increased visibility also means operational gaps become easier to identify.

This is why governance discipline becomes increasingly important after transformation programs begin consolidating systems and processes into a more unified enterprise platform.

Important Reality Check

There is no completely risk-free S/4HANA transformation.

Large enterprise programs almost always encounter:

  • unexpected dependencies,

  • process conflicts,

  • reconciliation challenges,

  • operational readiness gaps,

  • and business adaptation issues.

What separates successful programs is not the absence of problems.

It’s the organization’s ability to:

  • identify risks early,

  • maintain operational visibility,

  • coordinate effectively,

  • and adapt quickly throughout the transformation lifecycle.

Conclusion

From my experience, SAP S/4HANA transformations succeed when organizations treat them as enterprise-wide operational transformations instead of traditional ERP upgrade projects.

Technology enables modernization.
But governance, operational alignment, business readiness, and data discipline determine how successful that modernization becomes after go-live.

As S/4HANA adoption continues growing globally, organizations that focus equally on technical execution and operational resilience will be far better positioned for long-term transformation success.

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I
entry.content:
Great post — building an open-source enterprise Kafka adapter for SAP BTP Cloud Integration is a very practical move. It directly addresses the need for a flexible, reusable way to connect SAP integration flows to Kafka-based event streaming, instead of relying only on heavy custom code or rigid point-to-point integrations.

What stands out most is the focus on making Kafka integration feel enterprise-ready in Cloud Integration: reliable messaging, clean adapter packaging, and a reusable approach that can scale across multiple iFlows and landscapes. That’s exactly the kind of tooling SAP integration teams need as event-driven architecture becomes more common.

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I
entry.content:
@venkatamandavilli 

Thank you — that framing is exactly right.

One decision worth sharing openly: during development, I received strong technical insights suggesting to extend the adapter beyond TCP Kafka to also cover AMQP, MQTT, and HTTPS natively within the same component.

I rejected it immediately.

Not because it was technically impossible — but because it would have created exactly the kind of Frankenstein adapter that the SDIA architecture was designed to prevent. One of the core principles of SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture) is that governance, stability, and the non-proliferation of responsibilities must reflect not only inside the iFlow layer, but equally in the adapter layer itself.

An adapter that tries to speak TCP Kafka, AMQP, MQTT, and HTTPS at the same time is not an enterprise component — it is a maintenance liability with four failure surfaces instead of one. It violates the single-responsibility principle at the infrastructure level, and it makes governance, versioning, and troubleshooting exponentially harder for the teams who will maintain these integrations in production.

The correct architectural answer is: let Kafka be the universal bus. AMQP, MQTT, and HTTPS sources feed into Kafka upstream. The adapter does one thing — consume from Kafka reliably, convert, and deliver. SAP CPI does the orchestration. Each layer owns its responsibility. Nothing bleeds into anything else.

That discipline is what makes the difference between tooling that scales across enterprise landscapes and tooling that becomes technical debt six months after go-live.

Appreciate the feedback — this kind of conversation is exactly why this is being built in the open.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition: Automating WHT for intercompany AP/AR postings
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I would like to ask for guidance on automating withholding tax determination for intercompany transactions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

In my scenario, an intercompany sales document is created in the issuing company code, and the corresponding AP invoice is automatically posted in the receiving company code via IDoc. I am looking for a way to determine withholding tax automatically for these intercompany postings.

The requirement is that some intercompany transactions between the same entities are subject to withholding tax, while others are not. I am considering both:

  •             withholding tax at invoice, and
  •             withholding tax at payment.

I also need to understand the AR side of the process, because in the country version involved, assigning a withholding tax type to a condition type on the issuing side does not appear to be supported.

My questions are:

1. Is there any standard SAP approach in S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition to automate WHT determination for intercompany AP postings triggered by IDoc?

2. Is there any supported design for handling the AR side when the country version does not support assigning WHT type to a condition type?

3. If standard configuration is not sufficient, is there any recommended extensibility or workaround in Public Edition to determine whether WHT should apply at invoice or payment time?

I am not looking for a manual workaround. The requirement is to keep the process automated at posting time.

Any guidance, examples, or references to SAP standard best practice would be appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: SAP HANA High Availability (...Well, my version) on AWS Using EventBridge and Lambda
entry.content:
Great practical take on HA for SAP HANA on AWS. The post shows how you can combine Amazon EventBridge + Lambda to detect EC2 failures (stopping, stopped, terminated) in sub-seconds and trigger recovery actions, complementing traditional HANA SR-based HA with cloud-native automation.

What stands out is the hybrid monitoring approach:

  • EventBridge handles instant EC2-level failure detection (OS panic, hypervisor failure, hard stop).

  • A custom CloudWatch metric covers HANA process-level failures that EC2 monitoring can’t see.

This is a smart, lightweight way to add an extra layer of resilience on top of standard HA/DR setups, especially for teams that want to avoid heavy clustering complexity while still achieving fast failover.

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI
entry.content:
Very strong pattern. The post shows how to design immutable Kafka topic routing in SAP BTP Cloud Integration using SDIA (SAP Digital Integration Architecture) with ODCP (Orchestration Domain‑Centric Pattern):

  • Immutable iFlow topic routing per domain: each domain has its own package and Kafka topic, and the routing from iFlow to topic is fixed and not changed at runtime.

  • SDIA + ODCP structure:

    • SDIA defines the layered architecture.

    • ODCP (Layer 3) governs Integration Suite directly: one package per domain, every artifact organized by domain, and routing rules that are stable and versioned.

This gives you:

  • Clear domain boundaries and less coupling between teams.

  • Predictable, audit‑friendly event flows (topics don’t change dynamically).

  • A clean foundation for event-driven architectures using the SAP CI Kafka adapter.

Great reference for anyone building scalable, domain-driven Kafka-based integrations on BTP.

entry.title: EML Get Permission from SAP BO ( Billing )
entry.content:
Hello,

I have developed a custom app on the public cloud ( developer extensibility ).

I use the I_BILLINGDOCUMENTTP-CreateFromExternalData action (SAP Business Accelerator Hub - I_BILLINGDOCUMENTTP)
Furthermore I have set up two roles, one being able to execute the function via the Sales-Billing Document catalog (user "write"), the other being only able to create data in my app but not trigger the function above (user "read").
(Billing Document | SAP Help Portal)
If the read user performs the action, the eml fails and i can catch the error. (so far so good)
My intention now is to check if he is allowed to use the action via the global features method and disable the button if he is not.

I know that there is the GET PERMISSION ONLY GLOBAL AUTHORIZATION .... feature, but it returns true for all inputs on my end.
If I check the SAP implementation of I_BillingDocument~CreateFromExternalData, i can see, that the global authtorization method is not implemented and instead works with authorization context.
I can see there and in the business catalog for "write" (SAP_BCR_SD_BIL_DOC_PC) the required authorization objects.
Some of the authorization objects are not cloud ready and therefore cannot be used.

So my question is:
How do I check if the user can use the CreateFromExternalData action without using it in the first place?

entry.title: Join the SAP SuccessFactors AI User Group
entry.content:
AI is transforming HR faster than ever and staying connected to the conversation matters.

The new SAP SuccessFactors AI User Group is a global, customer-only space designed to help HR professionals stay engaged in AI discussions year-round through peer collaboration, shared insights, and direct access to SAP experts.

Inside the group, customers can:

  • Ask questions and exchange best practices with peers in a GDPR-protected environment (S-user/Universal ID required)
  • Engage directly with SAP HCM AI experts and consultants
  • Discover AI use cases, tips, and ongoing discussions without waiting for the next webinar or event
  • Help build a growing knowledge base the broader customer community can learn from

Whether you're just beginning your AI journey or looking to expand adoption across your organization, this group provides an always-on space to learn, collaborate, and stay informed.

Watch this short 90-second overview demo and join the user group to explore the experience firsthand.

We look forward to seeing you in the conversation.

entry.title: Re: Annotation @UI.selectionVariant for quantity field
